text: The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure of the colon, creating a small window posterior to the pancreas and splenic vessels, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach. Anteriorly, a similar view is obtained for controlling the splenic inflow with a stapler. Using a Warshaw-type technique, the splenic vessels are stapled while preserving the short gastric vessels to maintain partial spleen perfusion. The pancreas is demonstrated to be mobile and flipped over, with mobilization continuing from lateral to medial. The splenic artery is isolated, clips are placed proximally, and it is divided using the Thunder Beat device.

The surgeon observes the spleen, noting its dusky appearance but preserved superior pole, avoiding splenectomy. The splenic vein insertion into the superior mesenteric vein at the portal confluence is visible, indicating medial progress. The ligament of Treitz is mobilized from the mesenteric side, facilitating later dissection. The patient is repositioned for a supine approach, and the lesser sac is entered through the gastrocolic omentum, exposing the anterior surface of the pancreas. The pancreas is mobilized from lateral to medial, and the anterior surface of the duodenum and pancreatic head are identified. The beginnings of a Kocher maneuver are performed, mobilizing posterior and lateral duodenal attachments. The common bile duct and gallbladder are visible, and middle colic vessels are controlled using the Thunderbeat.

The surgeon skeletonizes the superior mesenteric vein, using a clip that evulses a draining vein. Despite bleeding, the field is cleaned, and bleeding is controlled with energy. The gastrocolic trunk is partially ligated and clipped at the level of the SMV, then divided using cautery. The insertion of the splenic vein into the superior mesenteric vein is isolated and ready to be stapled, with the anterior surface of the SMV and the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck clearly identified.

The Nathanson liver retractor is inserted and fastened to improve visualization. The surgeon dissects the stomach and creates a hole in the pars flaccida, identifying the proximal duodenum based on the vein of Mayo. The proximal duodenum is divided using a surgical stapler, and the stomach and a small portion of the duodenum are tucked into the left abdomen. The pancreas is flipped over, revealing the portal vein. Partial mobilization of the gallbladder and cystic duct is performed, facilitating the skeletonization of the bile duct and identification of the hepatic artery. The gastroduodenal artery is isolated and divided using clips and the Thunderbeat. The anterior surface of the portal vein is skeletonized up into the portal hepatus, with the hepatic artery and bile duct visible. Dissection begins at the uncinate process tethered to the superior mesenteric vein and artery, avoiding cystic components posteriorly. The camera is brought in laterally from the right side to complete the Cochrane maneuver, revealing the pulsing inferior vena cava and aorta, along with the insertion of the left renal vein into the IVC.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery to the left of the ligament of Treitz and divides the bowel, delivering the proximal jejunum into the right abdomen through the mesenteric window. The uncinate process and pancreatic cyst remain as the only points of attachment. Cystic components with small branches are tethered to the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), extending posteriorly. The splenic vein is mobilized off the cystic areas, facilitating lateral deflection of the uncinate process to the right. Smaller vessels are clipped, and the Thunder Beat is used for further mobilization, with precautions taken to cool the instrument using fat and fluid. The superior mesenteric artery is visible posterior to the SMV, and the surgeon carefully avoids cystic components during dissection.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the cystic components and uncinate process from the superior mesenteric artery using the Thunder Beat, periodically cooling the blade with serosanguinous fluid. The superior mesenteric artery is visible, pulsing alongside the pancreatic cystic components. The bile duct and gallbladder remain attached, with the bile duct located just superior to the Thunder Beat. The final attachments of the bile duct to the pancreas and uncinate process are severed, followed by a cholecystectomy. The bile duct is divided and stapled, measuring approximately five millimeters in diameter. The gallbladder and pancreas specimen are placed in a retrieval bag, and the small bowel is oriented for anastomosis in the right upper quadrant. A small enterotomy is made for the bilioenteric anastomosis, using a running suture technique with a four-oh braided suture due to the duct size.

The surgeon ties down the anastomosis securely, inspecting for any biliary leaks using a clean white sponge, which shows no evidence of leakage. A Jackson Pratt drain is placed around the anastomosis site. The specimen is extracted through a small supraumbilical incision, and an end-to-side duodenal jejunostomy is performed, preserving the pylorus.
